title: Adaptive Clifford+T Decomposition of Large Toffoli Gates with One Clean Ancilla
authors: Abhoy Kole, Majd Assaad, Till Schnittka, Rolf Drechsler
abstract: Multi-controlled Toffoli gates are fundamental building blocks in quantum computation, with applications in quantum arithmetic, simulation, and search algorithms. In fault-tolerant architectures, their realization is constrained by the high cost of non-Clifford resources, particularly in terms of T-count and T-depth. Recent advances have demonstrated that the use of ancillary qubits, relative-phase Toffoli gates, and dynamic circuit techniques can substantially reduce this overhead.   In this work, we investigate the decomposition of large Toffoli gates using 3- and 4-input relative-phase Toffoli gates in the presence of a single clean ancilla and conditionally clean ancillas. We derive explicit resource bounds for Clifford+T implementations incorporating dynamic-circuit-based uncomputation and measurement-conditioned corrections. Our analysis emphasizes T-depth reduction under fixed CX and T-count overhead, ensuring relevance for near-term devices. We show that introducing 4-input relative-phase Toffoli gates enables significant T-depth reductions through enhanced parallelism while maintaining favorable ancilla requirements. We further validate our theoretical results through experimental evaluation and comparative analysis with existing approaches.
summary: This paper proposes a method to decompose large multi-controlled Toffoli gates into Clifford+T gates more efficiently by using 4-input relative-phase gates and dynamic circuits. By leveraging intermediate measurements and conditional corrections, the researchers significantly reduce the T-depth of these gates, which is a critical bottleneck in fault-tolerant quantum computing.
main_problem: Efficiently decomposing large multi-controlled Toffoli gates into the Clifford+T gate set while minimizing T-count, T-depth, and CNOT overhead in fault-tolerant architectures.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that using 4-input relative-phase Toffoli gates and dynamic circuit techniques (measurement-conditioned corrections) significantly reduces T-depth, achieving up to a 41.74% reduction for large gates without increasing T-count or CX-count.
method: The study utilizes 3- and 4-input relative-phase Toffoli gates combined with dynamic circuit techniques, such as intermediate measurements, qubit resets, and measurement-conditioned feed-forward operations to replace costly uncomputation.
model_or_system: The work focuses on fault-tolerant quantum computing architectures using the Clifford+T universal gate set, specifically targeting the implementation of multi-controlled X gates ($C_n X$).
key_observables: T-count, T-depth, and CX-count (CNOT gate count).
important_parameters: Number of control qubits ($n$), availability of a single clean ancilla, and the use of conditionally clean ancillas.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the availability of at least one clean ancilla and the ability to perform intermediate measurements and classical feed-forward (dynamic circuits).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Clifford+T decompositions for relative-phase gates; Figure 2 compares different 5-input Toffoli realizations; Figures 3 and 4 illustrate dynamic implementations using measurement-conditioned operations; Figures 5-7 demonstrate large-scale gate realizations and T-depth reduction via parallelization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multi-controlled Toffoli decomposition, presents new decomposition techniques using 3- and 4-input relative-phase gates, details the implementation of dynamic circuit techniques for uncomputation, provides a comparative analysis of resource bounds, and validates results through experimental/numerical evaluation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Relative-phase Toffoli gate implementations: (a) the CC(iX) gate, (b) its Clifford+T decomposition, and (c) the Clifford+T realization of the C3(iX) gate.
Fig. 2: (a) The realization of a 5-input Toffoli (C4X) gate with one clean ancilla, implemented through two equivalent decompositions: (b) with four CC(iX) gates, and (c) with two C3(iX) gates.
Fig. 4: A dynamic implementation of the C4X gate using one C3(iX) gate together with measurement-conditioned op- erations: CX·CC(iZ) when the ancilla outcome is 1, and CS†
Fig. 3: A dynamic realization of the C4X gate employing three CC(iX) gates and a conditional CZ operation triggered by the ancilla measurement.

Fig. 5: A dynamic realization of the C15X gate with one clean ancilla, using a network of 25 CC(iX) gates, one CCX gate, and a conditional CZ operation triggered by ancilla measurement, derived from [8].
